question: How does ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) work in Swift?
answer:
ARC tracks reference counts for class instances at compile time. The compiler inserts retain/release calls so each instance is freed exactly when its last strong reference drops.

Key rules:
• Strong (default) — increments retain count. Owns the object.
• Weak — does NOT increment retain count. Auto-niled when target is freed. Must be `var Optional`.
• Unowned — non-incrementing, non-optional. Crashes if accessed after target is freed. Use only when lifetimes are guaranteed.
• Closures capture by strong reference by default — `[weak self]` / `[unowned self]` capture lists are how you avoid cycles.
• Value types (struct/enum) are copied; ARC doesn't apply.

ARC is deterministic — finalization runs immediately when refcount hits 0 (no GC pause).

question: What is a retain cycle? How do you detect and prevent them?
answer:
A retain cycle is two (or more) reference-counted objects holding strong references to each other so refcount never hits zero. The objects leak.

Common sources:
• Closures captured strongly by their owner (delegates, completion handlers, Combine sinks, NotificationCenter blocks).
• Bidirectional class relationships (parent ↔ child) without weak/unowned.
• Repeated NSTimer / DispatchSourceTimer holding `target = self`.

Detect:
• Xcode's Memory Graph Debugger — pause, click the icon, look for cycles (purple icons indicate leaks).
• Instruments → Leaks template.
• `deinit print` smoke test — does the object's `deinit` ever fire?

Fix: `[weak self]` capture lists, `weak` properties, invalidate timers in deinit.

question: How do you profile an iOS app with Instruments?
answer:
Instruments is Apple's profiler suite, attached to a running app via Xcode (Product → Profile, or ⌘I).

Common templates:
• Time Profiler — sampling-based CPU profile. Find heavy methods; collapse by frame; look at the heaviest stack.
• Leaks — finds memory leaks (objects with no path to a root).
• Allocations — track how memory is used over time. Find spikes and growth.
• Network — request timings, payload sizes.
• Energy Log — battery impact (crucial for long-running apps).
• Hangs — captures main-thread stalls > 250ms (great for jank).
• Animation Hitches — frame drops on UIKit / SwiftUI.
• MetricKit — gather signals from real users without Instruments (crashes, hangs, battery, disk).

Profile on a real device with a release build (Profile config). Simulator timings are misleading.

question: How do you keep the main thread responsive in iOS apps?
answer:
Main thread is responsible for UIKit/SwiftUI updates AND every user interaction. Anything > 16ms blocks a frame; > 250ms is a hang. Strategies:

• Move work off-main: Task / async let, DispatchQueue.global, Operation queues.
• Use async/await — automatic suspension means you don't accidentally block.
• Compute-heavy in background: image decoding, JSON parsing, large lists. Apply results on main.
• Defer expensive work: do it on `viewDidAppear` (not viewDidLoad/willAppear) or after first paint.
• Image pipelines: preload thumbnails on background queue, hand decoded UIImage to main.
• Avoid synchronous APIs on main: Core Data fetches, file I/O.
• Use Instruments → Hangs / Time Profiler with Main Thread filter to spot offenders.

question: How do you optimize UITableView / UICollectionView scrolling performance?
answer:
Smooth 60/120fps scrolling needs every frame's work to fit in 16ms (8ms on ProMotion). Common optimizations:

• Cell reuse — never create cells per visible row.
• Pre-decode images off-main (CGImageSourceCreateImageAtIndex with shouldCacheImmediately).
• Layer-back complex cells (`layer.shouldRasterize` only for static, expensive layers).
• Avoid layoutIfNeeded inside cellForRow — let layout pass run once after configuration.
• Use diffable data sources to avoid full `reloadData`.
• Estimated row heights — provide a fast estimate so UIKit doesn't measure all upfront.
• Cell registration with content configurations (UIListContentConfiguration) — cheaper than custom cells with many subviews.
• Self-sizing cells: combine UICollectionViewCompositionalLayout `.estimated` with content configurations.
• Avoid offscreen rendering: rounded corners on UIImageView via `cornerRadius` are cheaper than masks.
• Profile with Instruments → Animation Hitches / Time Profiler.

question: How do you analyze and improve app launch time?
answer:
Launch is the user's first impression. Measured in two phases:

• Pre-main — dyld, runtime init, Objective-C +load methods, static initializers. Reduce by avoiding +load, minimizing big static init, trimming linked frameworks.
• Post-main → first useful frame — `application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:)` + scene/UI setup until your initial screen renders.

Measure with:
• Xcode → Profile → Time Profiler with App Launch template.
• Instruments → App Launch.
• MetricKit `MXAppLaunchMetric` (real users).
• `os_signpost` around your own startup phases.

Optimizations:
• Defer heavy init (analytics SDK, image cache) to after first frame.
• Lazy-load services with @Lazy or first-use injection.
• Reduce dynamic frameworks (each costs dyld time).
• Pre-warm critical caches on a background queue, not synchronously on main.
• Use Instruments `Time Profiler` to find synchronous I/O / Core Data ops on main.
